hook: Colonial police HQ, magistracy and prison reborn as an arts compound
facts: Sixteen buildings built between 1841 and 1925 off Hollywood Road
Victoria Prison, completed 1842, was Hong Kong's first; decommissioned 2006
Reopened 2018 after an 1.8 billion HKD Jockey Club revitalisation
w: Sixteen granite buildings off Hollywood Road that for a century formed a single machine of colonial law: police station, magistracy and the Victoria Prison of 1842, where Ho Chi Minh once did time. Decommissioned in 2006, scrubbed up by 2018, the cell blocks and parade ground now hold galleries. Justice repurposed as leisure.

hook: Cobalt-blue balcony tenement saved by its own tenants
facts: Balcony-type tong lau built 1922-27 on Stone Nullah Lane, a Grade I building
Painted blue reputedly because that was the surplus paint the works depot had
Restored 2016 letting residents stay; won a UNESCO conservation award in 2017
legend: They say the walls went blue only because government leftover paint happened to be that colour
w: A four-storey balcony tenement from the 1920s, painted vivid cobalt supposedly because surplus government paint happened to come in that shade. Once a Chinese hospital, it survived the wrecking ball when tenants fought to stay put, winning a UNESCO award in 2017. Rare wooden tong lau living, not embalmed.

hook: Hong Kong Island's largest temple, guarded by a giant bronze deity
facts: Built by locals in 1863 on Lung On Street, declared a monument in 2019
Houses a three-metre Ming-dynasty bronze of Pak Tai cast in 1604
Once ran a free school and community office within its halls
w: Up the steps off Queen's Road East stands the biggest temple on the island, raised by Wan Chai locals in 1863 around a three-metre bronze of Pak Tai, the martial Northern Emperor, cast back in 1604. Antique iron bells, a former charity school in the side halls. A quiet giant hemmed in by glass towers.

hook: Smoke-blackened temple to the gods of literature and war
facts: Built between 1847 and 1862, among the oldest on Hong Kong Island
Honours Man, god of literature, and Mo, god of war, with brush and sword
Once served as an unofficial courthouse for the Chinese community
w: Giant incense coils hang from the rafters, smouldering for weeks under prayer cards, hazing the gilt interior of one of the island's oldest temples, raised around 1847 for Man the god of letters and Mo the god of war. Chinese elders once settled disputes here as a shadow courthouse. Step in from Hollywood Road and the century falls away.

a: Shek Kip Mei, Sham Shui Po
hook: Last H-shaped resettlement block, now a housing museum
facts: Built after the 1953 Shek Kip Mei fire that left 50,000 squatters homeless
Sole surviving Mark I resettlement block, with shared communal toilets
Reopened 2013 as a youth hostel and free museum of public-housing life
w: After a Christmas fire in 1953 burned out 50,000 squatters overnight, the colony threw up emergency H-shaped blocks; this lone survivor keeps the cramped cubicles and shared latrines that housed them. Now a hostel and free museum recreating a 1950s flat where whole families lived in one room. Hong Kong's housing story, ground zero.

hook: Edwardian red-brick slaughterhouse turned artists' colony
facts: Served as an abattoir from 1908 until 1999 on Ma Tau Kok Road
Only pre-WWI cattle depot left in Hong Kong, a Grade II building
Converted to studios for around twenty art groups in 2001
w: Low red-brick sheds with cast-iron tethering rings, built in 1908 to slaughter cattle and pigs until the knives stopped in 1999. The city's last pre-war abattoir reopened in 2001 as warren of studios and tiny galleries, the only working creatures now artists. Ghosts of the killing floor under fresh paint.

hook: Four-storey curved verandah shophouse of a bus tycoon
facts: Built around 1931 for Lui Leung, a co-founder of Kowloon Motor Bus
Ground floor held the Lui Seng Chun bonesetter and herbal medicine shop
Grade I building, now a Baptist University Chinese medicine clinic and tea shop
w: A wedge-shaped 1931 tong lau with sweeping verandahs, raised by bus magnate Lui Leung above his family's bonesetting and herbal shop, the trade that gave the building its name. The medicine counter closed after his death in 1944. Restored by a university, it again pours herbal tea downstairs. East-meets-west grandeur on a traffic island.

hook: Vertiginous canyon of stacked flats housing ten thousand people
facts: Part of an E-shaped cluster of five 1960s blocks once called Parker Estate
Original developer vanished mid-build; five others finished the project
Around 2,200 units hold roughly 10,000 residents; a film backdrop for Transformers
w: Five 1960s blocks fused into one E-shaped wall of flats, begun after the original developer simply vanished and finished by whoever stepped in. Stand in the central courtyard and the windows tower in dizzying tiers, ten thousand lives stacked overhead. Ghost in the Shell filmed here. Density made vertical and faintly terrifying.
